refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, teleoperation overhead, and safety system energy draw. The refined FR establishes the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) as the verification anchor and acknowledges that multiple subsystems contribute to this outcome.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a baseline energy consumption profile across representative worksite scenarios (load classes, terrain, distance, cycle time). Measure total energy input (fuel or electrical) and total mass moved (ton) over the evaluation period. Calculate energy_per_ton and compare against E_target. Disaggregate energy consumption by subsystem (propulsion, teleoperation, safety services, auxiliary) to identify efficiency drivers and bottlenecks.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle propulsion system shall minimize energy consumption per unit distance traveled under load.
FR-EE-002: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, compute, latency compensation) relative to direct operation.
FR-EE-003: The safety services subsystem shall execute safety-critical functions without exceeding a defined energy budget.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to the operator and site manager.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall support operator training and behavior optimization to achieve energy efficiency targets.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that monitors real-time energy consumption across all subsystems, compares against a dynamic efficiency baseline, and provides corrective feedback to the operator and autonomous control layers to optimize energy per ton.

DP-B: Optimize vehicle drivetrain efficiency (engine/motor, transmission, hydraulics) through component selection and control tuning; independently optimize teleoperation and safety subsystem energy budgets; aggregate subsystem efficiency targets to meet overall energy_per_ton target.

DP-C: Establish a hierarchical energy allocation strategy: allocate energy budget to propulsion (primary), teleoperation (secondary), safety (non-negotiable minimum), and auxiliary (residual); enforce budget compliance through real-time throttling and mode switching.

DP-D: Combine vehicle efficiency optimization (DP-B approach) with operator-in-the-loop energy awareness and incentive mechanisms (gamification, efficiency scoring, training) to drive behavioral change and achieve energy_per_ton target through human factors.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish a hierarchical energy allocation strategy with real-time budget enforcement and mode switching, coupled with subsystem-level efficiency optimization (DP-B) and operator feedback mechanisms (DP-D). This hybrid approach decouples safety-critical energy requirements from discretionary loads, ensures predictable energy behavior, and provides multiple levers (hardware efficiency, allocation policy, operator behavior) to achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

potential_sources: Energy allocation policy (DP-C) is independent of vehicle drivetrain design (DP-B subsystem optimization) but requires interface contracts on energy consumption profiles and mode switching latency.
Operator feedback and training (DP-D) depends on real-time energy visibility from the allocation system (DP-C) but does not create functional coupling; it is an enabler.
Safety services energy budget (non-negotiable minimum in DP-C) must be validated against actual safety function energy requirements; this is a constraint, not a coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is the primary control mechanism; DP-B and DP-D are supporting strategies. The design is decoupled if energy allocation policy is agnostic to the specific efficiency mechanisms employed by subsystems, and if operator feedback does not alter safety-critical energy allocations.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: E_target is defined by the customer and is technically achievable with the selected vehicle platform and teleoperation architecture.
ASSUMPTION: Real-time energy consumption measurement is feasible across all subsystems with acceptable sensor accuracy and latency.

RISK: If E_target is set below the minimum energy required for safe operation (safety services + propulsion minimum), the requirement becomes infeasible. Mitigation: Validate E_target against safety energy budget early.

RISK: Operator behavior and training effectiveness are difficult to predict and control. Mitigation: Establish baseline operator efficiency metrics and design training program with measurable outcomes.

RISK: Teleoperation overhead (communication, compute, latency compensation) may consume significant energy, reducing the margin available for propulsion optimization. Mitigation: Quantify teleoperation energy footprint and establish separate efficiency targets for tele-operated vs. autonomous modes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define the energy allocation hierarchy as a state machine with clear mode transitions (e.g., Normal, Efficiency, Safety-Priority, Degraded). Specify the energy budget for each subsystem as a function of worksite scenario (load class, terrain, cycle time) and operational mode. Define the feedback control law that adjusts operator throttle authority or autonomous speed based on real-time energy consumption vs. budget.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. At this level, the IM assignment for CR-NGHE-003 (energy efficiency KPI) is the NGHE Solution itself, as energy efficiency is a system-level emergent property that depends on the coordinated behavior of all subsystems. The NGHE Solution IM is responsible for defining and enforcing the hierarchical energy allocation strategy (DP-C), aggregating energy consumption telemetry from subsystems, computing energy_per_ton metrics, and providing visibility and control interfaces to operators and site managers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time energy consumption data from Vehicle (fuel/electrical input, propulsion efficiency), Teleoperation (communication and compute energy), and Safety Services (sensor and actuation energy). Telemetry also includes operational context (load, distance, cycle time, terrain) needed to compute energy_per_ton and compare against dynamic baselines.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite task definitions (load class, destination, cycle time target) from Site Manager. Task parameters inform the energy allocation strategy and efficiency baseline for the current operation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits alerts if energy allocation strategy detects that safety-critical energy budget is at risk or if mode switching (e.g., to Safety-Priority mode) is triggered. Also receives safety constraints that define the non-negotiable minimum energy budget for safety services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication channel for energy efficiency metrics, operator feedback, and efficiency scoring to be transmitted to Site Manager dashboard and Tele-operator console. Also receives operator commands and mode preferences that influence energy allocation policy.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural container for system-level energy efficiency management. The hierarchical energy allocation strategy, real-time budget enforcement, and energy_per_ton computation are system-level concerns that require visibility across all subsystems. Minimal diffusion: energy allocation policy is localized to a dedicated energy management module within NGHE Solution; subsystem-level efficiency optimizations are delegated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services but coordinated via energy budgets.

Vehicle is responsible for propulsion efficiency optimization (DP-B subsystem optimization) and for reporting energy consumption telemetry. However, Vehicle alone cannot satisfy CR-NGHE-003 because energy efficiency is a system-level KPI that depends on teleoperation overhead and safety services energy draw. Assigning the entire DP-C (hierarchical energy allocation) to Vehicle would create diffusion: Vehicle would need to enforce energy budgets for subsystems outside its scope (Teleoperation, Safety Services), violating separation of concerns.

Teleoperation is responsible for minimizing its own energy overhead (DP-B subsystem optimization for teleoperation) and for reporting energy consumption. However, Teleoperation cannot own the system-level energy allocation strategy (DP-C) because it does not have visibility into Vehicle propulsion or Safety Services energy requirements. Assigning DP-C to Teleoperation would create high diffusion and coupling.

Safety Services is responsible for defining its own energy budget (non-negotiable minimum in DP-C) and for reporting energy consumption. However, Safety Services cannot own the system-level energy allocation strategy because it does not have authority over Vehicle or Teleoperation energy budgets. Assigning DP-C to Safety Services would violate the principle that safety-critical functions should not be burdened with system-level optimization logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-003 because: (1) Energy efficiency is a system-level emergent property that cannot be achieved by optimizing subsystems in isolation; (2) The hierarchical energy allocation strategy (DP-C) requires system-level visibility into energy consumption across all subsystems and authority to enforce budget constraints; (3) Real-time energy_per_ton computation and feedback to operators and site managers are system-level concerns; (4) Subsystem-level efficiency optimizations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are delegated to respective subsystems but coordinated via energy budgets defined at the NGHE Solution level. This assignment minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: RISK: If the NGHE Solution IM is implemented as a thin orchestration layer without sufficient computational resources or real-time capability, it may not be able to enforce energy budgets with acceptable latency. Mitigation: Allocate sufficient compute and network bandwidth to the NGHE Solution energy management module; consider edge computing or distributed control if latency becomes critical.

RISK: If subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) do not provide accurate or timely energy consumption telemetry, the NGHE Solution cannot compute reliable energy_per_ton metrics. Mitigation: Establish telemetry contracts with clear accuracy, latency, and completeness requirements; validate telemetry data quality during integration testing.

RISK: If the energy allocation strategy is too rigid or does not adapt to changing worksite conditions, operators may override or disable it, defeating the purpose. Mitigation: Design the allocation strategy with operator override capabilities and adaptive baselines that learn from historical data.

RISK: Coupling between energy allocation policy and safety-critical functions: if energy budget enforcement inadvertently reduces safety system responsiveness, the design violates safety principles. Mitigation: Establish safety-critical energy budget as a hard constraint that is never throttled; validate that mode switching does not compromise safety.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution IM boundary to explicitly define the Energy Management Module as a dedicated subsystem within NGHE Solution. This module should own the hierarchical energy allocation strategy, real-time budget enforcement, energy_per_ton computation, and operator feedback mechanisms. Define clear interfaces between Energy Management Module and Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services for energy budget allocation, telemetry reporting, and mode switching. Establish a formal contract for energy consumption telemetry (accuracy, latency, completeness) and energy budget enforcement (response time, override mechanisms).

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period) is directly addressed by the selected DP (hierarchical energy allocation strategy with real-time budget enforcement and subsystem optimization). The DP provides a logical solution path that decouples safety-critical energy requirements from discretionary loads and enables multiple levers (hardware efficiency, allocation policy, operator behavior) to achieve the target. The DP is implementable and testable against the FR acceptance criterion.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If the hierarchical energy allocation strategy is implemented across multiple modules (e.g., Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) without a central coordinator, the design will suffer from inconsistent budget enforcement and lack of system-level visibility. Mitigation: Centralize energy allocation policy in NGHE Solution Energy Management Module; subsystems report consumption and request budget adjustments, but do not independently enforce allocation.

DIFFUSION RISK: If energy_per_ton computation is duplicated across multiple modules (e.g., Vehicle dashboard, Tele-operator console, Site Manager portal), metrics may diverge and create confusion. Mitigation: Compute energy_per_ton once in NGHE Solution Energy Management Module and distribute results via Network Link interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution IM provides strong containment for the hierarchical energy allocation strategy and system-level energy management. Subsystem-level efficiency optimizations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are appropriately delegated to respective subsystems but coordinated via energy budgets. The assignment minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and support the DP implementation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable through direct measurement of energy consumption and mass moved over the evaluation period. The NGHE Solution IM provides the necessary telemetry collection, computation, and reporting infrastructure to support verification. Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is objective and measurable. Verification plan should include baseline profiling, subsystem-level efficiency testing, and end-to-end system testing across representative worksite scenarios.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of CR-NGHE-003 to NGHE Solution IM is clear and unambiguous. The NGHE Solution owns the system-level energy efficiency KPI and is responsible for defining and enforcing the hierarchical energy allocation strategy. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own subsystem-level efficiency optimizations and energy budget compliance. The allocation is consistent with the refined FR and selected DP.

recommended_revisions: Establish a formal Energy Management Module specification within NGHE Solution that details the hierarchical energy allocation strategy, real-time budget enforcement algorithm, energy_per_ton computation, and operator feedback mechanisms.
Define energy budget contracts for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems, including budget allocation, telemetry reporting requirements, and mode switching protocols.
Establish a verification and validation plan that includes baseline energy profiling, subsystem-level efficiency testing, and end-to-end system testing across representative worksite scenarios.
Define operator training and behavior optimization program to support achievement of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through human factors (FR-EE-005).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is a system-level KPI that depends on the coordinated behavior of multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). The refined FR and selected DP provide a clear logical solution path (hierarchical energy allocation strategy), but the implementation requires decomposition into subsystem-level functional requirements and design parameters. The five sub-FRs identified in the refined FR (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) represent the next level of functional decomposition and must be allocated to respective subsystems and analyzed for coupling, containment, and feasibility. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to ensure that subsystem-level designs are consistent with the system-level energy efficiency strategy and that all sub-FRs are satisfied.
decision.justification_detail: The NGHE Solution IM assignment is appropriate for the system-level energy efficiency KPI, but the implementation of the hierarchical energy allocation strategy (DP-C) requires detailed design of the Energy Management Module and its interfaces with subsystems. The sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) must be decomposed further to define subsystem-level design parameters and implementation modules. For example, FR-EE-001 (vehicle propulsion efficiency) must be decomposed into design parameters for engine/motor efficiency, transmission efficiency, and hydraulic efficiency, each of which may be assigned to specific vehicle components. Similarly, FR-EE-002 (teleoperation energy overhead) must be decomposed into design parameters for communication efficiency, compute efficiency, and latency compensation, each of which may be assigned to specific teleoperation subsystems.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle propulsion system shall minimize energy consumption per unit distance traveled under load.
FR-EE-002: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, compute, latency compensation) relative to direct operation.
FR-EE-003: The safety services subsystem shall execute safety-critical functions without exceeding a defined energy budget.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to the operator and site manager.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall support operator training and behavior optimization to achieve energy efficiency targets.

assumptions: E_target is defined by the customer and is technically achievable with the selected vehicle platform and teleoperation architecture.
Real-time energy consumption measurement is feasible across all subsystems with acceptable sensor accuracy and latency.
Operator behavior and training effectiveness can be influenced through feedback mechanisms and incentive structures.
Teleoperation overhead (communication, compute, latency compensation) is quantifiable and can be optimized independently of propulsion efficiency.
Safety-critical energy budget is non-negotiable and takes precedence over discretionary energy allocation.
The hierarchical energy allocation strategy can be implemented with acceptable latency and computational overhead.
open_risks: E_target may be set below the minimum energy required for safe operation, making the requirement infeasible.
Teleoperation energy overhead may consume significant energy, reducing the margin available for propulsion optimization.
Operator behavior and training effectiveness are difficult to predict and control.
Energy allocation strategy enforcement may inadvertently reduce safety system responsiveness.
Subsystems may not provide accurate or timely energy consumption telemetry, compromising energy_per_ton computation.
Operators may override or disable energy allocation strategy if it is perceived as too restrictive.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined E_target value and evaluation period.
Baseline energy consumption profile across representative worksite scenarios (load classes, terrain, distance, cycle time).
Energy consumption breakdown by subsystem (propulsion, teleoperation, safety services, auxiliary).
Telemetry accuracy and latency specifications for energy consumption measurement.
Safety-critical energy budget requirements from Safety Services subsystem.
Teleoperation energy overhead quantification (communication, compute, latency compensation).
Operator training and behavior optimization program design and effectiveness metrics.
Verification and validation plan for energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion.
